title: Cantonese Beef Pan-Fried Noodles

overview.description: Crisp pan-fried egg noodles topped with tender beef, vegetables and a glossy Cantonese-style sauce.

ingredients: 350g beef steak, such as sirloin or rump, thinly sliced against the grain
1 tbsp cornflour mixed with 2 tbsp cold water
method: Mix the beef with the light soy sauce, cornflour and 1 tsp neutral oil.
Leave to marinate for 15–20 minutes.
Prepare the noodles according to the packet instructions, taking care not to overcook them.
Drain thoroughly.
Heat 1½ tbsp of the pan-frying oil in a large non-stick frying pan over medium-high heat.
Add the noodles and spread them into an even layer.
Cook without disturbing until the underside is golden and crisp.
Carefully turn the noodles or slide them onto a plate and invert them back into the pan.
Add another 1½ tbsp oil if necessary.
Cook until the other side is also lightly crisp.
Transfer the noodle cake to a serving plate.
Heat a wok or large frying pan over high heat.
Add a little neutral oil if necessary.
Stir-fry the beef in batches until browned but still tender.
Remove and set aside.
Add the onion and green pepper.
Stir-fry until beginning to soften.
Add the garlic and ginger.
Cook for 30 seconds.
Add the beef stock, oyster sauce, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar and white pepper.
Bring to a simmer.
Stir in the cornflour slurry gradually until the sauce thickens to a glossy consistency.
Add the pak choi and bean sprouts.
Cook briefly until just tender but still slightly crisp.
Return the beef to the sauce and heat through.
Pour the beef and vegetable sauce over the crisp noodle cake.
Serve immediately.

learning.did-you-know: Cantonese pan-fried noodles are often served as a crisp noodle cake topped with a separate stir-fried meat and vegetable sauce. The sauce gradually softens the noodles where it touches them while the edges remain crisp.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to create a crisp noodle cake in a frying pan and how a thickened sauce creates a contrast between crisp and tender textures.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Cantonese noodle dish that introduces a completely different texture from chow mein and lo mein by combining crisp pan-fried noodles with a separate glossy beef and vegetable sauce.
chapman-tips: Use a good non-stick frying pan for the noodle cake.
Drain the noodles very thoroughly before pan-frying.
Don't move the noodles too much while they are crisping.
Cook the beef in batches so it browns rather than steams.
Slice the beef against the grain for the most tender texture.
Add the sauce to the noodles just before serving so they retain some crispness.
The noodle cake does not need to be perfectly round or completely intact.
Fresh egg noodles generally give a better texture than dried noodles for this dish.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The crisp noodles lose their texture when stored and reheated.
notes.leftovers: The beef and sauce can be refrigerated and reheated separately, but the noodles are best freshly prepared.
